title: The Chesapeake deserves a longer view
content: To the editor: After thirty-five years spent reviewing land-management policy, I have learned that the Chesapeake does not respond to one budget cycle or one administration. The oyster reefs and the tidal marshes keep a slower calendar than we do. If we want the bay our grandchildren fish in to resemble the one we knew, we must fund restoration on a horizon measured in decades, not in the next appropriation. Steady, patient work has always served this watershed better than any grand announcement.
excerpt: A letter to the editor arguing that Chesapeake restoration must be funded on a horizon of decades, not budget cycles.

title: What the Civilian Conservation Corps still teaches us
content: In the 1930s the Civilian Conservation Corps put young men to work planting trees, cutting trails, and building the state parks many Marylanders still enjoy. It was public policy that produced something you could walk through. I have been reading the camp records this spring, and what strikes me is how much lasting good came from work that was, at bottom, unglamorous and shared. There is a lesson in that for anyone drafting conservation policy today.
excerpt: Reflections on the Civilian Conservation Corps and the durable public good of shared, unglamorous conservation work.

title: On the county's proposed changes to the West Annapolis setbacks
content: To the editor: The proposed revisions to shoreline setbacks in West Annapolis deserve a careful hearing before they are adopted. Having lived on Munroe Court for many years, I have watched what heavy rain does to our streets when the ground can no longer hold it. Buffers are not red tape; they are the reason our basements stay dry. I would urge the council to keep the existing margins and to listen to the neighbors who have watched the water rise.
excerpt: A letter urging the county council to preserve shoreline setbacks in West Annapolis for stormwater's sake.

title: Notes on reading Wallace Stegner's 'Beyond the Hundredth Meridian'
content: I finished Stegner's account of John Wesley Powell this week and set it down changed. Powell understood, more than a century ago, that the arid West could not be settled as if it were the wet East, and that policy divorced from the land it governs will fail. I spent a career in Interior watching that same truth get relearned the hard way. A fine book, and a humbling one for anyone who ever wrote a regulation.
excerpt: Book notes on Wallace Stegner's account of John Wesley Powell and policy that respects the land it governs.

title: Public lands are a promise, not an inventory
content: To the editor: I read with concern the argument that our federal lands should be judged mainly by the revenue they can produce. That was never their purpose. A national forest or a wildlife refuge is a promise one generation makes to the next, and its value shows up in clean water, working ecosystems, and a place to stand quietly and watch the herons. I spent my career on land-management policy, and I never once met a spreadsheet that could measure that.
excerpt: A letter making the case that public lands are an intergenerational promise, not a revenue inventory.

title: A word of thanks for the Annapolis library's tutoring program
content: To the editor: Every Thursday morning I sit down with adults working toward their GED at our public library, and every Thursday I am reminded what a quiet civic treasure that building is. The volunteers, the staff, the patrons who share a table without a word of complaint about the noise from our corner. Before the next budget season arrives, I hope our neighbors will remember that the library is not a line item to trim but a public good worth defending.
excerpt: A draft letter thanking the Annapolis library's GED tutoring program and urging support for the library.

title: In defense of the county stormwater fee
content: To the editor: Runoff into the Severn does not fix itself. The stormwater fee is a small price for a bay our grandchildren can fish.

content: A reflection on the Civilian Conservation Corps and the Maryland state parks its camps built.

title: A quiet word for the library volunteers
content: To the editor: The people who keep our branch running deserve more notice than they will ever ask for.

title: On reading four books a month in retirement
content: Curiosity did not retire when I did. A short essay on the discipline of a reading life.
